summary:
The primary reasons are due to nitrous oxide having insufficient potency at achieving required depths of anesthesia without causing risks from excessively high concentrations; also challenging to maintain proper oxygen levels throughout surgery which requires additional supportive measures beyond what can be provided by itself effectively making it unsuitable standalone choice for complete General Anesthesia regimen.
Nitrous Oxide lacks sufficient efficacy and potencies compared to other gases thus unable provide requisite depth & duration of surgical induced unconsciousness independently while simultaneously managing critical parameters such as blood gasses thereby rendering it impractical solo application within context defined here i.e., total intravenous anaesthesia setup intended primarily towards induction/adjunctive roles rather than mainstay solution capable sustaining entire procedure uninterrupted safely efficiently across wide range conditions encountered clinically today . Summary:

- Insufficient potency makes nitrous oxide ineffective for inducing and maintaining deep anesthesia.
- Challenges in maintaining appropriate oxygen levels limit its use as a single-agent general anesthetic.
